All the mixes for the hydraulic pressure test were pre- pared in a 100 L mixer according to the standard EN 196-1. The yield stress of the studied concretes was measured using a concrete rheometer equipped with a Vane tool. The vane geometry used in this study consisted of four 10 mm thick blades around a cylindrical shaft of 120 mm diameter. The blade height was 60 mm and the vane diameter was 250 mm. The gap between the rotating tool and the external cylinder was equal to 90 mm, which is sufﬁciently large to avoid any scaling effect due to the size of the gravel ( D max = 10 mm). The results for the ﬁbered concretes are not given in Table 2 due to the lack of geometry of our rheom- eter (120 mm diameter) regarding the ﬁbre size (35 mm), which can make the obtained data reﬂects some measurement artefact. Flexural tests were carried out at 28 days of age. To obtain the necessary ﬁbre distribution, a manual ﬁbre-counting method was used to measure ﬁbre density distribution occurring at the cracked section of the specimen, following a ﬂexural test subjected to four points loading. In order to asses the ﬁbre-alignment, a so-called orientation-factor a (0 6 a 6 1) was introduced, as proposed by Soroushian and Lee [32]. If all ﬁbres were oriented perpendicular to a cut surface, the average number of ﬁbres in such a cross-sec- tion would be equal to the area of the ﬁbres in the cross-section (Ac) divided by the area of one ﬁbre (A f) multiplied with the volume percentage of the ﬁbre (V f). The orientation-factor a is deﬁned as the ratio of the number of ﬁbres actually counted (N ) in a given cross-section and this theoretical number (N th) with only perpen- dicular ﬁbres. a ¼ N Nth ¼ N Af Vf Ac ð2Þ Ac is the transverse section of concrete, Ac =1 5 /C2 15 = 225 cm 2, Af is the section of a ﬁbre, Af ¼ p d2 f 4 ¼ 0:0048 cm2, Vf is the volume frac- tion of ﬁbres ( Vf = 0.5%) and N is the number of ﬁbres crossing a gi- ven section. For a = 0, the ﬁbres are not oriented perpendicularly to the cross-section; this is equivalent to concrete without ﬁbres. For a = 1, all the ﬁbres are oriented perpendicularly to the cross-sec- tion. The lower this factor is, the larger is the average inclination of the ﬁbres from the perpendicular direction to the cut surface. These equations describe the situation without taking boundary conditions into account. Dupont and Vandewalle [24] reported an increase in the orientation factor between 0.5 and 0.58 for their particular test arrangement (geometry, ﬁbre length from 40 to 60 mm). The orientation coefﬁcient a as a function of the yield stress is represented in Fig. 5 below. Each point is the average of three spec- imens. In the case of ﬂuid concrete (OC and SCC with 45 and 36 Pa yield stress, respectively), the ﬁbres have a preferred orientation with values of 0.45 and 0.57 respectively for the coefﬁcient a and ﬁbres are uniformly distributed, which enables an increased level of friction between matrix and ﬁbre. On the contrary, for high- strength concrete (high yield stress of 120 Pa), a = 0.30, meaning that the ﬁbres are rather gathered as clusters and badly dispersed in the internal concrete structure and hardly any ﬁbre orientation can be observed. This supports the observations made on the ﬂuid model (see Fig. 3 ). 3. Inﬂuence of ﬁbre orientation on the compression and the ﬂexural strengths 3.1. Compression test The compression tes

beneﬁcial use, is activated only after the appearance of cracking, particularly at the post-peak stage. The pre-peak behaviour in compression is then identical to that of concrete without ﬁbres. On the contrary, the presence of ﬁbres greatly improves the post-peak behaviour and reduces substan- tially the descending slope of the stress–strain curve by softening the material when approaching failure as seen in Fig. 7. This obser- vation is even more pronounced in the case of high-strength concrete. 3.2. Flexure test In ﬂexure, the ﬁbres are efﬁcient when they are oriented in the direction of the tensile stresses. Such efﬁciency is even greater for high-strength concrete, since the bond between the ﬁbres and the paste matrix is of a better quality [10]. Flexure strength tests were performed on 15 /C2 15 /C2 70 cm prismatic specimens, under four- point ﬂexure on a span of 60 cm with a displacement control at a rate of 0.004 mm/s. The applied load and deﬂection data were elec- tronically acquired at an acquisition frequency of 10 Hz. All mea- surements were made on three specimens. The failure of steel ﬁbre-reinforced concrete is not a (tendency to become?) brittle at maximum load. A load corresponding to the ﬁrst crack may be identiﬁed at this stage, but its magnitude de- pends on the sensitivity of the technique used to detect cracks. Moreover, because crack propagation is restricted by ﬁbres, there may be more than one peak on the curve before the maximum is reached (curve FROC and FRSCC in Fig. 10 ). It can be noted that the rheology effect, particularly the yield stress, is signiﬁcant. For the ordinary and self-compacting con- cretes with a low yield stress, the distribution of ﬁbres is more homogeneous (uniformly distributed) and well oriented in the direction of the tensile stresses created by ﬂexure (see Fig. 8 a). On the contrary, in high-strength concretes with a high yield stress, the ﬁbre orientation is random and their distribution is imperfect, as illustrated in Fig. 8b, showing that the better the concrete ﬂows, the more the ﬁbres align. The efﬁciency of ﬁbres is clearly evident in the case of FRC and FRSCC; in both cases, the ultimate load has increased compared to the load at ﬁrst crack, by comparison to that in high-strength concretes in which ﬁbres are almost inefﬁcient; an increase of 72%, 58% and 81% were recorded for FROC, FRHSC and FRSCC respectively. Flowability of ﬁbre-reinforced concrete and its effect on the mechanical properties of the material Bensaid Boulekbache a, Mostefa Hamrat a, Mohamed Chemrouk b, Soﬁane Amziane c,* a Département de Génie Civil, Université de Chlef, Algérie/LIMATB, Université de Bretagne Sud, France b Université des Sciences et Technologie Houari Boumediene, Alger, Algeria c Clermont Université, Université Blaise Pascal, EA 3867, Laboratoire de Mécanique et Ingénieries, BP 10448, F-63000 Clermont-Ferrand, France article info Article history: Received 10 July 2009 Received in revised form 11 February 2010 Accepted 11 February 2010 Available online 17 March 2010 Keywords: Concrete Rheology Yield stress Fibre Orientation Strength abstract Fibre distribution is an important factor, affecting both the fresh- and hardened-state properties of con- crete. The paper describes a study in which the ﬁbre distribution and orientation were investigated by means of a suitable technique, by using a translucent ﬂuid model with a yield stress (Carbopol 940). The observation conﬁrmed the ability of the developed method to provide data on the orientation and distribution of steel ﬁbres within concrete. It was showed that orientation and distribution are dependent on the yield stress of the ﬂuid material. Concrete specimens, containing 0.5% of 35 mm long hooked steel ﬁbres, were also cast and tested under four-point loading, in order to study the correlation between rhe- ology, ﬁbre distribution and the mechanical properties of steel ﬁber-reinforced concrete. The results con- ﬁrm that the rheology of concrete has an important inﬂuence on the orientation of the ﬁbres. The ﬂexural strength depends on the ﬁbre distribution and orientation and is signiﬁcantly improved when the ﬁbres are oriented in the direction of the tensile stresses fresh (concrete with good workability). On the con- trary, for concrete with poor workability, an inadequate orientation of ﬁbres occurred, leading to a poor contribution of the ﬁbres to the ﬂexural behaviour of the tested specimens, despite the relatively higher compression strength of the tested concrete material. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Fibre concrete has known many developments and numerous studies have been carried out on this material during the last three decades, in order to overcome the tension weakness common to all types of concretes. This notion of tension weakness as opposed to compression strength is maintained in high-strength concrete. Fur- thermore, with the increase in compression strength, the concrete material becomes brittle and relatively less ductile, with relatively more catastrophic failures. The brittle behaviour of high-strength concrete can be overcome by the addition of steel ﬁbres in the con- crete mix. These ﬁbres reinforce and compensate for the brittleness of concrete by stitching the micro-cracks and the macro-cracks and taking up the subsequent tensile strains acting on concrete [1]. Among other objectives, adding ﬁbres to a concrete matrix in- creases the toughness or energy-absorbing capability of the hard- ened concrete material, to make it more suitable for use in structures subjected to impacts and earthquake loads. Reinforcing high-strength concrete with steel ﬁbres therefore seems an efﬁcient process, conferring to the hardened material a relatively better ductility, particularly as the steel ﬁbres develop a better bond with the dense and compact hardened cement ma- trix

It should be noted that the compression strength does not have a great effect in ﬂexure tests and that the ﬂexural strength does not increase in proportion with the compression one, leaving the ﬂex- ural strength of high-strength concrete just slightly higher than that of ordinary concrete. To sum up, it could be said that the orientation of ﬁbres and the rheology inﬂuence signiﬁcantly the ﬂexural strength of beams. For the behaviour in bending, the issue when evaluating the use of ﬁbres in concrete is not only the increase in the ﬁnal ﬂexural strength, but also the ductile behaviour which can make the use of this concrete material worthwhile and structurally interesting. A cracked concrete continues to support further increases in load- ing without worsening the crack width through ﬁbre crack stitch- ing and through the deformation of the ﬁbres, which causes increased crushing and splitting of the matrix. At the ﬁnal stage, failure is very ductile and soft since most of the energy is absorbed by the deformed ﬁbres. For the composite ﬁbered concrete, more than one load peak may occur, one at the end of the cement matrix contribution and the others when the ﬁbres reach their ultimate capacity. To compare the energy absorbed by the three types of concrete, their ductility indexes have been calculated according to ASTM C1018 [34]. The ductility indexes I 5, I10, I20, I30, I50 and I100 repre- sent the energy absorbed for different values of deﬂection, calcu- lated as the ratio of the area generated by the load–deﬂection curve corresponding to 3, 5.5, 10.5, 15.5, 25.5 and 55.5 times the deﬂection corresponding to the ﬁrst crack. The witness specimens did not contain ﬁbres and hence failed immediately after ﬁrst cracking. Consequently, the ductility index for these concretes is equal to 1. The ﬁrst cracking is deﬁned in the load–deﬂection curve as the point at which the ﬁrst deviation from linearity occurs. However, locating the point of ﬁrst crack is subject to human error [35,36]. In the present study, the use of a measuring system based on image analysis obtained by a digital recording camera enabled us to de- tect ﬁrst cracking with a great precision, to measure the crack opening and to follow the progressive cracking process (see Fig. 9). It has thus allowed us to correct any possible errors in cal- culating the ductility indexes. Ductility indexes I 5 and I10 calculated according to ASTM stan- dards do not describe accurately the behaviour in ﬂexure; they are often in the instable zone. It is better to consider higher ductil- ity indexes corresponding to higher deﬂections, such as I 50 and I100, for a complete description of the structural behaviour in ﬂexure. The results concerning the average values of the measured load at ﬁrst cracking, the peak load together with the corresponding mid-span deﬂection and orientation factor, are reported in Table 3. The ductility was in general higher for concrete with good workability (FRSCC and FROC) as shown in Figs. 10 and 11 . This shows the signiﬁcant effect of ﬁbre distribution and orientation by comparison to the effect of the compression strength of con- crete. At high matrix strengths (FRHSC in Fig. 10b), there is usually a steeper and sudden drop in load-carrying capacity after the occurrence of peak load. However, in an efﬁcient ﬁbre-reinforced concrete (FROC and FRSCC in Fig. 10 b), the release of energy will be offset by an effective bridging of ﬁbres. During the inelastic stage between

4. Conclusions The main scope of the paper was to examine the characteriza- tion of ﬁbre orientation in a ﬂuid with a yield stress model and to establish the relationships between rheology (yield stress), ﬁbre distribution and mechanical properties. From this study, the fol- lowing conclusions can be drawn: About the effect of the rheology on the orientation of the ﬁbres: – The use of a ﬂuid model (Carbopol 940) containing ﬁbres was found to be adaptable for the prediction of ﬁbres distribution and orientation. Such predictions are affected by the yield stress of the ﬂuid material. The ﬁbre orientation is very sensitive to the yield stress and the conditions of ﬂow conﬁnement (walls effect). With a low yield stress, the ﬁbres were well oriented per- pendicularly to the ﬂuid ﬂow (Carbopol or concrete). – Fibres align with the ﬂow of the fresh concrete; when the con- crete ﬂows through a mould, a ﬂow proﬁle develops from the frictional restraint of the mould walls. – In the case of ﬂuid concrete (SCC with 36 Pa yield stress), the ﬁbres have a preferred orientation with values of 0.57 for the coefﬁcient orientation-factor a (magnitude characterizing ﬁbre-alignment) and ﬁbres are uniformly distributed, on the contrary, for concrete with a high yield stress of 120 Pa, orienta- tion-factor a is found equal to 0.30. – About the effect of ﬁbre orientation on the mechanical behaviour: – In compression, the presence of ﬁbres in concrete reduced the compression strength by around 6% for ﬁbre-reinforced self-com- pacting concrete, 7% for ordinary ﬁbre-reinforced concrete and 10% for high-strength ﬁbre-reinforced concrete. The better distri- bution of the ﬁbres in SCC, inducing more homogeneity and fewer defects in the matrix, is certainly the origin of this result. – In ﬂexural tests, the use of a measuring system based on image analysis obtained with a digital recording camera enabled us to detect with a great precision the ﬁrst cracking, to measure the crack opening and to follow the progressive cracking process. This measurement method led to a precise determination of the specimen’s ductility. – The ﬁbre orientation factor plays a prominent role and governs the response of the material under ﬂexure. The ductility and ﬂexural strength is largely improved for SCC (orientation factor a = 0.57) compared to a less workable concrete (high yield stress). This shows the important effect of the ﬁbre orientation compared to the effect of the compression strength of concrete. – It was also observed that a low yield stress concrete leads to a good alignment of the ﬁbres showing multiple cracking. This is the consequence of the activation of the longitudinal transfer loading as it can be observed in traditional reinforced concrete. On the contrary, for a concrete with high yield stress, all defor- mations seem localized into a single crack only. The ﬁbres are not sufﬁciently oriented and connected together to ensure a lon- gitudinal transfer loading. References [1] Amziane S, Loukili A. Etude expérimentale du comportement des bétons à hautes performances renforcés de ﬁbres d’acier sous chargements statique et cyclique. Mater Struct 1999;32(5):348–53. [2] Nawy EG. Fundamentals of high-performance concrete. 2nd ed. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.; 2001. [3] Balaguru PN, Shah SP. Fiber-reinforced cement composites. New York: McGraw-Hill Inc.; 1992. [4] Naaman AE. /C15 Numerous investigations dealing with structural applica- tions of steel-ﬁbre concrete can be found in the literature. It has been shown that the replacement of vertical stirrups by steel ﬁbres provides effective reinforcement against shear failure [12,13]. Casanova has shown that a quantity of 1.25% of steel ﬁbres in volume is mechanically equivalent to 1.1% of conventional steel reinforcement [14]. The parameters guaranteeing better structural properties for the ﬁbres are the nature of the ﬁbres, their quantity, their orienta- tion and the ﬂowability of the fresh concrete [15]. The orientation of the ﬁbres inside the matrix is affected by a number of parameters, essentially the geometry of ﬁbres and their interaction effects (ﬁbres-aggregates-formwork), the ﬂowability of concrete, the means of pouring and compacting the concrete, the geometry of the concrete shafts (free surface, two or four bound- aries) and their dimension. From our point of view, the rheology parameters of the ﬂowing material, particularly the yield stress and the wall effect generated by the geometry of the formwork, are the greatest inﬂuences on the orientation of the ﬁbres within the fresh concrete. The distribution and orientation of ﬁbres is, in turn, the parameter which inﬂuences the most the ductility of ﬁbre concretes. The contribution of the ﬁ- bres to the ductility depends on their distribution within the con- crete element and especially their orientation. A good distribution is required to achieve optimum beneﬁts from the ﬁbres [16–18]. Concrete is a concentrated suspension of solid particles (aggre- gates) in a viscous liquid (cement paste). Fresh cementitious mate- rials, as many materials, behave as a ﬂuid with a yield stress, which is the minimum stress needed to initiate ﬂow. Various methods are used to measure and deﬁne rheological properties and a number of proposed models describe the rheolog- ical behaviour of fresh concrete as suspensions. The most com- monly used is the Bingham model, which is given by: s ¼ s0 þ l0 _c ð1Þ In which s is the shear stress (Pa), s0 the yield stress (Pa), l0 the plastic viscosity (Pa.s) and _c the shear rate (1/s). For concrete, the parameters s0 and l0 in the Bingham equation are not constant in time, due to the development of thixotropy and loss of workability [19,20]. On the other hand, the slump test, the most common empirical test for fresh concrete, does not give any value of a rheological parameter at all. Its result cannot be expressed in physical rheolog- ical units, though it has also proved through the years to be efﬁ- cient for classifying different materials in terms of their ability to ﬁll-up a formwork. From a practical point of view, yield stress may be the most interesting value where ﬁlling or passing ability is concerned, whereas plastic viscosity may be associated to the velocity at which a given concrete will ﬂow once the material begins to ﬂow. Concrete placement, which includes transportation, pumping, cast- ing and vibration, is affected by the yield stress and plastic viscos- ity of fresh concrete. In practice, yield stress is the most important parameter for formwork ﬁlling. The viscosity of the material will only play a role on the time needed to obtain a horizontal surface [21,22]. Sepehr [23] shows that in shear tests carried out in a Newtonian ﬂuid, the orientation of ﬁbres is mainly controlled by the deforma- tion of the ﬂuid. They also show that viscosity does not have, in fact,

Estellé analysis makes it possible to compute the radius of the sheared material inside the gap. The vane geometry used in this study consisted of four 2 mm thick blades around a cylindrical shaft of 6 mm diameter. The blade height was 37 mm and the vane diameter was 25 mm. Once the solution is ready, steel ﬁbres with hooked ends are added in a proportion of 0.5%. The ﬁbres have a constant length of 35 mm and an aspect ratio (ﬁbre length/ﬁbre diameter) of 65. Fig. 1 shows the sizes and shape of these ﬁbres. The liquid contain- ing the ﬁbres is made to ﬂow through a funnel (fountain ﬂow). Two types of ﬂow are then studied: /C15 A free surface ﬂow or radial ﬂow, as shown in Fig. 2 . /C15 A ﬂow conﬁned by two linear walls, called channel ﬂow as in Fig. 3 . The preliminary observations show that for a yield stress equal to 25 Pa, the orientation of ﬁbres, in the case of free surface ﬂow, is mainly perpendicular to the ﬂow direction and radial orientation of ﬁbres is observed (Fig. 2 a). In the case of a channel ﬂow, the perpendicular orientation of ﬁbres is disturbed by the conﬁning effect of the side walls. The wall effect depends on the width of the ﬂow channel (Fig. 3a). The reori- entation of the ﬁbres in the longitudinal direction increases as the width of the beam tends towards the size of a ﬁbre. For a yield stress equal to 70 Pa, the orientation of ﬁbres is ran- dom and accompanied by groupings of ﬁbres and by the formation of ﬁbre balls (a balling-up effect) near the ﬂow centre (Figs. 2b and 3b). It should be noted that the vibration forces up the ﬂow of the ﬂuid and at the same time makes it rotate so that the ﬁbres natu- rally reorient perpendicularly to the forced ﬂow direction. But why do the ﬁbres align? The ﬁbres align because of the ﬂow velocity proﬁle. In the case of a channel, the velocity of ﬂow varies from zero at the walls to a maximum along the centreline of the channel. A simpliﬁed illustra- tion is shown in Fig. 4 . Different ﬂow velocities affect the ﬁbres and may cause them to rotate in such a way that the ﬁbres reorient perpendicularly to the ﬂow direction (see Figs. 4a and 4b). The ﬂuid exerts hydrodynamic forces on the ﬁbres. In the presence of relative velocities in trans- lation and rotation, the ﬂuid exerts forces and moments on the ﬁ- bres. The effect is stronger at higher ﬂow velocity or when the velocity can affect the ﬁbre for longer time (i.e. lower viscosity or longer ﬂow duration) [16]. 2.2. Application to concrete The rheology and hence the ﬂowability, of ﬁbre concrete is a major concern for many users. In effect, the ease of placing of ﬁbre concrete has a direct impact on the orientation of ﬁbres inside the material’s structure and consequently on its structural efﬁciency once the hardened concrete is loaded. As seen previously, the distribution of ﬁbres is modiﬁed accord- ing to the rheology of the cement matrix. In a ﬂuid concrete, that is a concrete having a lower yield stress, the ﬁbres have a great mobility within the cement matrix and can move and orient easily under the effect of a light external vibration. On the contrary, in a stiff concrete, that is a concrete having a higher yield stress, there is a risk of formation of ﬁbre balls; this limits the movement of ﬁbres and may even block them. In this case, a homogeneous repartition of ﬁbres becomes impossible to achieve. The direct consequence of this anisotropy is the reduction of the number of ﬁbres crossing a given section. Table 1 Yield s

To further study the effect of rheology and hence ﬂowability, on the orientation of ﬁbres inside the concrete structure, three con- crete mixes were manufactured: an ordinary ﬂuid concrete (OC), a self-compacting concrete (SCC) and a plastic high performance concrete (HSC), with the proportions given in Table 2 . The cement used in this study was a CEM I 52.5 N of a ﬁneness expressed trough a speciﬁc surface of 3520 cm 2/g and a density of 3160 kg/m3. Hooked-end steel ﬁbres were used, 35 mm in length, 0.55 mm in diameter and 65 in aspect ratio. These ﬁbres are avail- able in bundles, which were ﬁbrillated with water-soluble glue to ensure immediate dispersal in the concrete during mixing. A single ﬁbre dosage rate of 40 kg/m 3 (0.5% per volume of concrete) was used for all the mixes. The sand and coarse granulate had mini- mum/maximum sizes of 0/4 mm and 4/10 mm, respectively. The high-range water-reducing admixture (HRWRA) was a polycarbox- ylate-based admixture. In the production of this concrete, the constituent materials were initially mixed without ﬁbres. The ﬁbres were then added in small amounts to avoid ﬁbre balling and to produce a concrete with uniform material consistency and good workability. The measurement of the slump ﬂow indicates the free deforma- bility of the mixture. The ‘slump ﬂow’ is the average diameter of the horizontal ﬂow measured in two perpendicular directions after lifting Abrams’cone (cone size 100–200 mm diameter, height 300 mm). The compactness of the SCC matrix, due to the greater amount of ﬁne and extra-ﬁne particles, may improve interface zone prop- erties [31] and consequently also the ﬁbre–matrix bond, leading to an enhanced post-cracking toughness and energy absorption capacity. In case of SCC, no sign of segregation was detected, the mixture showed good homogeneity and cohesion. The concrete was placed in three equal layers into the beam mould.
